items.q: Can a definite integral give a negative value, even when integrating a function from left to right?
items.correct: Yes — if the function is below the x-axis over the interval, the integral is negative.
items.misconception: Definite integrals are always positive because area cannot be negative.
items.random_wrong: Definite integrals are negative only when the limits are reversed.
items.why: Students equate integrals with geometric area and forget the integral measures signed area.

items.q: What is the sum of the interior angles of any pentagon?
items.misconception: 360 degrees, because all polygons' angles sum to one full turn.
items.random_wrong: 180 degrees, the same as a triangle.
items.why: Students remember a single 'angles add up to' figure and apply it universally, or confuse interior with exterior angles (which do sum to 360).

items.q: If 5 pens cost £3.50, how much do 7 pens cost?
items.correct: £4.90 — find the unit cost (£0.70 per pen), then multiply by 7.
items.misconception: £5.50, by adding £2 to £3.50 because there are 2 more pens.
items.why: Students apply additive thinking (more pens means add more pounds) instead of multiplicative reasoning via the unit rate.
The answer examiners want is: a^2 Var(X) + b^2 Var(Y). “a Var(X) + b Var(Y)” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: normal. “uniform” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Exact answer: 4. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
